section.title: The King of Glory
section.sectionRationale: A processional entrance liturgy, likely sung as the ark of the covenant was carried up to Zion, moving from a declaration of God's ownership of the whole earth, through the same entrance question asked in Psalm 15, to a triumphant, antiphonal call for the ancient gates to lift up their heads so the King of glory may enter. Its closing question and answer, 'Who is this King of glory', has been read since the earliest church as anticipating Christ's ascension and enthronement.
section.thesis: The earth and everyone in it belongs to the LORD who alone may ascend His holy hill with clean hands and a pure heart, and the psalm's climactic call for the ancient gates to admit the King of glory anticipates the triumphant entrance and enthronement of Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the LORD's ownership of the whole earth; the entrance question of who may ascend His holy hill, answered by clean hands and a pure heart; and the closing antiphonal call for the gates to lift up their heads so the King of glory may enter.

verses.text: The earth is the LORD's, and the fullness thereof, the world and all who dwell therein.
For He has founded it upon the seas and established it upon the waters.

units.label: Who may ascend the hill of the Lord?

verses.text: Who may ascend the hill of the LORD? Who may stand in His holy place?

He who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not lift up his soul to an idol 
or swear deceitfully.
He will receive blessing from the LORD and vindication from the God of his salvation.
Such is the generation of those who seek Him, who seek Your face, O God of Jacob. {{selah|Selah}}

units.label: Lift up your heads, O gates

verses.text:
Lift up your heads, O gates! Be lifted up, O ancient doors, 
that the {{kavod|King of Glory}} may enter!

Who is this King of Glory? The LORD strong and mighty, 
the LORD mighty in battle.

Lift up your heads, O gates! Be lifted up, O ancient doors, 
that the King of Glory may enter!
Who is He, this King of Glory? The LORD of Hosts, He is the King of Glory. Selah

selah.gloss: A musical or liturgical notation of uncertain precise meaning, likely marking a pause, rest, or moment for reflection or instrumental interlude within the psalm's singing.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, splendor, the visible weightiness of God's presence and majesty. The 'King of kavod' is the King whose very presence carries the weight of divine glory, a title the New Testament applies to the risen and ascended Christ, 1 Corinthians 2:8, James 2:1.

basic.context: This psalm opens by declaring that the whole earth and everyone in it belongs to the LORD, its Creator and founder. It then asks the same searching entrance question found in Psalm 15, who may ascend God's holy hill, and answers with a portrait of clean hands and a pure heart. The psalm closes with a dramatic, likely antiphonal, procession scene: a call for the ancient gates themselves to lift up their heads so the King of glory may enter, followed by the question, repeated twice, 'Who is this King of glory', answered each time by naming the LORD, strong, mighty, and the LORD of Hosts.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm was likely composed for a specific historical procession, perhaps when David brought the ark of the covenant into Jerusalem, 2 Samuel 6, but its closing scene of gates opening for the entering King of glory has, from the earliest church onward, been read as prophetically pointing beyond that original event to Christ's ascension and enthronement, without denying its original historical setting.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm marks its central turning points with selah, and its climactic title for God, the King of kavod, glory, describes the visible weightiness of divine majesty entering through gates that are called to lift up their heads, a title later applied by the New Testament directly to the risen and glorified Christ.
basic.typology: The ancient gates and doors commanded to lift up their heads for the entering King of glory function as a type of the heavens themselves opening to receive the ascending, exalted Christ, and of every barrier that must yield before His rightful lordship.
basic.christ: The early church consistently read this psalm's triumphant procession as anticipating Christ's ascension, the true King of glory entering into heaven's gates after His resurrection. Paul calls Jesus 'the Lord of glory' using this exact title, 1 Corinthians 2:8, and James similarly names Him 'the Lord of glory', James 2:1, applying this psalm's climactic title directly to Christ.
basic.god: God is shown as Creator and rightful owner of the whole earth, as the holy one who requires clean hands and a pure heart of those who would approach Him, and as the triumphant King of glory, strong and mighty, worthy of the grandest possible welcome.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 6:12-15 records David bringing the ark of the covenant up to Jerusalem, the probable original setting of this psalm. 1 Corinthians 2:8 and James 2:1 both call Jesus the Lord of glory, applying this psalm's title directly to Him. Ephesians 4:8-10 describes Christ's ascension far above the heavens.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's declaration that the whole earth belongs to the LORD, its entrance question requiring clean hands and a pure heart, and its climactic call for the ancient gates to admit the King of glory together anticipate the whole gospel story's completion: Christ's triumphant ascension and enthronement as the true King of glory, who alone grants sinners clean hands and a pure heart through His own righteousness.

context.literary: A processional entrance liturgy in three clear movements, cosmic declaration of ownership, verses 1-2, ethical entrance requirement, verses 3-6, and a dramatic antiphonal call and response at the gates, verses 7-10, likely sung by two choirs, one inside the gates and one accompanying the procession outside.
context.historical: Most likely composed for the occasion when David brought the ark of the covenant up to Jerusalem, 2 Samuel 6, or for its later installation in Solomon's temple, both moments when God's own presence, symbolized by the ark, entered the city gates in procession.
context.canonical: Closely paired with Psalm 15's nearly identical entrance question, and its title, King of glory, becomes, through New Testament application, one of the church's earliest and most exalted titles for the risen and ascended Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To celebrate God's sovereign ownership of all creation, to search worshipers' hearts with the same ethical entrance requirement as Psalm 15, and to dramatize, in worship, the triumphant entrance of the glorious King, likely at the historical procession of the ark into Jerusalem.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical or liturgical procession; its enduring, prescriptive teaching affirms both God's total ownership of creation and the genuine ethical requirement for approaching Him, while its climactic King of glory language carries forward typologically into New Testament application to Christ.

debates.question: What specific historical event, if any, originally occasioned this psalm's procession imagery in verses 7-10?

views.label: Composed for David's procession bringing the ark of the covenant into Jerusalem, 2 Samuel 6, the ark itself symbolizing God's throne and presence entering the city gates
views.case: The dramatic call for ancient gates to lift up their heads fits naturally with a historical procession where the ark, representing God's enthroned presence, was carried through the actual gates of Jerusalem for the first time under David.

views.label: Composed later for a recurring liturgical procession or for the dedication of Solomon's temple, functioning as a regularly repeated ritual rather than a single historical event
views.case: The psalm's antiphonal, call and response structure suggests it may have been designed for repeated liturgical use at temple festivals, celebrating God's kingship and presence on an ongoing basis rather than commemorating one unrepeated historical procession.

debates.bearing: Either historical setting preserves the psalm's essential theological meaning, that God's own glorious kingship enters His people's worship and history, and both readings leave room for the New Testament's further, typological application of the King of glory title to Christ's ascension and enthronement.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's selah marks its central pauses, and its climactic title, the King of kavod, glory, pictures the visible weight of divine majesty entering through gates commanded to lift up their heads, the very title, Lord of glory, the New Testament applies directly to the risen and exalted Christ.

typology.type: The ancient gates commanded to lift up their heads for the entering King of glory, verses 7-9
typology.fulfillment: Christ's ascension into heaven and enthronement at the Father's right hand, Ephesians 4:8-10, where every barrier yields before His lordship.

typology.type: The King of glory, strong and mighty, the LORD of Hosts, verses 8, 10
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, explicitly named the Lord of glory by both Paul and James, 1 Corinthians 2:8, James 2:1.

study.christ: The early church read this psalm's triumphant procession, gates commanded to lift up their heads for the entering King of glory, as anticipating Christ's ascension into heaven after His resurrection, His exaltation to the Father's right hand, and His final return in glory. Paul directly names Jesus 'the Lord of glory' using this psalm's exact title, 1 Corinthians 2:8, as does James, 'our glorious Lord Jesus Christ', James 2:1, showing the earliest church understood this psalm's climactic title as belonging fully to Christ.
study.god: God is revealed as Creator and rightful owner of the entire earth, as the holy God who requires genuine purity of those who approach Him, and as the triumphant King of glory, strong and mighty in battle, worthy of the grandest welcome creation can offer.

crossReferences.note: Records David bringing the ark of the covenant up to Jerusalem in procession, the probable historical setting behind this psalm's imagery.
Paul calls Jesus the Lord of glory, using this psalm's exact climactic title.
Describes Christ's ascension far above the heavens, the fulfillment of this psalm's picture of the King of glory entering in triumph.

study.oneStory: This psalm's declaration that the whole earth belongs to the LORD, its searching entrance question requiring clean hands and a pure heart, and its climactic call for the ancient gates to admit the King of glory all point forward to the completion of the gospel story: the ascension and enthronement of Jesus Christ, the true King of glory, who alone gives sinners the purity needed to stand in God's holy presence.

quiz.q: What does the psalm declare about the earth in verses 1-2?

options.text: That the earth and everyone in it belongs to the LORD, who founded it upon the seas and waters.
options.note: This opening declaration of God's total ownership grounds the psalm's entire message.

options.text: That the earth belongs jointly to all its human inhabitants equally.
options.note: The psalm attributes ownership specifically and solely to the LORD, not to humanity collectively.

options.text: That the earth is eternal and uncreated.
options.note: The psalm explicitly describes God founding and establishing the earth, implying it is created, not uncreated.

options.text: That only Israel's specific territory belongs to God, not the whole earth.
options.note: The psalm's language is universal, the earth and its fullness, not limited to a specific territory.

quiz.q: How does verse 4 answer the entrance question of who may ascend the LORD's hill?

options.text: The one who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not worship idols or swear deceitfully.
options.note: This ethical portrait closely parallels the answer given in Psalm 15.

options.text: Only those born into the priestly tribe of Levi.
options.note: The psalm's criteria are ethical and spiritual, not based on tribal descent.

options.text: Anyone who has offered the required number of sacrifices.
options.note: The psalm's answer centers on inward purity and honest dealing, not a quota of sacrifices.

options.text: Those who possess the greatest material wealth.
options.note: The psalm's criteria concern moral and spiritual purity, not material wealth.

quiz.q: What dramatic scene unfolds in verses 7-10?

options.text: A call for ancient gates and doors to lift up their heads so the King of glory may enter, followed by the repeated question, who is this King of glory.
options.note: This antiphonal call and response forms the psalm's dramatic, triumphant climax.

options.text: A description of the destruction of Jerusalem's gates by an invading army.
options.note: The scene describes gates being lifted up in triumphant welcome, not destroyed by an enemy.

options.text: A private prayer offered silently by a single worshiper.
options.note: The scene is public, dramatic, and communal, likely antiphonal between two choirs, not a private, silent prayer.

options.text: A list of specific temple furnishings and their measurements.
options.note: This is unrelated to the psalm's actual content, which is a triumphant procession scene, not architectural measurements.

quiz.q: How is the King of glory identified in verses 8 and 10?

options.text: As the LORD, strong and mighty, mighty in battle, and the LORD of Hosts.
options.note: Both answers to the repeated question name the LORD Himself with titles of strength and command.

options.text: As an unnamed future prophet.
options.note: The psalm names the King of glory explicitly as the LORD, not an unnamed prophet.

options.text: As the reigning human king of Israel at the time.
options.note: While a human king may have led the procession, the psalm's answer identifies the King of glory as the LORD Himself, not merely the human monarch.

options.text: As a foreign king conquering Jerusalem.
options.note: The scene celebrates the LORD's triumphant entrance, not a foreign conqueror's invasion.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply this psalm's title, King of glory or Lord of glory, to Christ?

options.text: Paul calls Jesus 'the Lord of glory' in 1 Corinthians 2:8, and James calls Him 'our glorious Lord Jesus Christ' in James 2:1, both using this psalm's exact title.
options.note: These direct New Testament uses of the title show the earliest church applying this psalm's climactic language to Christ.

options.text: The New Testament never applies any title from this psalm to Jesus.
options.note: This is incorrect; both Paul and James explicitly use the title Lord of glory for Jesus.

options.text: It is applied only to God the Father, never to Jesus.
options.note: The New Testament passages cited apply the title specifically and directly to Jesus Christ.

options.text: It is applied only in a symbolic sense with no real theological weight.
options.note: The title carries substantial theological weight, affirming Christ's full deity and glory, not a merely symbolic use.

questions: This psalm declares that the whole earth, not just sacred spaces, belongs to the LORD. How does remembering that everything you have and everywhere you go already belongs to God change the way you use your time, money, or possessions this week?

The entrance question asks for clean hands and a pure heart. Where do you sense your own hands or heart are not clean right now, and how do you need to bring that honestly before God rather than hiding it?

The psalm pictures a dramatic, joyful procession welcoming the King of glory. What would it look like for your own approach to worship this week to carry more of that sense of anticipation and celebration rather than routine?

The question 'Who is this King of glory' is asked twice and answered each time by naming God's strength and might. When you think about who God is, do strength and might come to mind readily, or do you tend to picture Him in smaller, more manageable terms?

The New Testament applies this exact title, Lord of glory, directly to Jesus. How does calling Jesus by this exalted, powerful title shape the way you pray to Him or trust Him with what currently feels too big for you to handle?

translationNotes.note: Selah likely marks a pause for reflection or musical interlude, appearing here after the description of those who seek God's face.
Lift up your heads, O gates pictures ancient city gates personified, called to open wide enough and high enough for a great and glorious king's procession to enter.

The source BSB text used an em dash in 'The LORD of Hosts, He is the King of Glory'; here it is rendered with a comma to preserve the sense without using a dash. King of Glory renders King of kavod, a title Paul and James both apply directly to Jesus Christ in the New Testament.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 24:1-10. The source text contained one em dash in verse 10, converted to a comma per house style; no em or en dashes remain in this file.
